agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation) with payment validation, retry logic, tier-specific grace periods, and free trial constraints.","bounded_context":"Subscription Billing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Subscription","definition":"A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, progressing through states: trial, active, past_due, cancelled."},{"term":"Payment Method","definition":"A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees. Has a status of valid or failed."},{"term":"Billing Cycle","definition":"The recurring period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when charges occur."},{"term":"Renewal","definition":"The automated process of charging a subscriber's payment method to extend the subscription for another billing cycle."},{"term":"Renewal Attempt","definition":"A single try to charge the payment method during the renewal window. Up to 3 attempts for standard tiers."},{"term":"Grace Period","definition":"A 14-day window granted to Premium tier subscribers before cancellation, replacing the 3-strike retry rule."},{"term":"Free Trial","definition":"A zero-cost introductory subscription period, limited to one active trial per email address."},{"term":"Premium Tier","definition":"A subscription level that receives preferential treatment during renewal failures (14-day grace period instead of 3 strikes)."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription with a known-bad payment method would create an immediately-corrupt state: an active subscription that cannot be billed. The system would be promising service it cannot collect revenue for. This is a precondition guard on a state transition, not a tunable policy — allowing it would violate the fundamental contract that active subscriptions have a viable billing path.","testable_assertion":"Given a payment method with status 'failed', any attempt to activate a subscription using that payment method MUST be rejected and the subscription MUST NOT transition to 'active'."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 7-day lead time is a business policy choice that balances retry window length against billing timing. A VP could reasonably change this to 5 or 10 days based on payment processor SLAs, retry success rate data, or customer feedback. The system would not be in a corrupt state if this window changed — it is an operational tuning parameter.","testable_assertion":"Given an active subscription, the renewal process MUST be initiated exactly 7 days before the current billing cycle's end date."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The threshold of 3 attempts is a business policy decision reflecting a tolerance level for payment failures. This number could be changed to 2 or 5 based on churn analysis, payment processor retry recommendations, or competitive benchmarking. The system state remains consistent regardless of the specific threshold. Note: the existence of a cancellation-after-exhaustion mechanism could be argued as invariant, but the specific count of 3 is clearly policy.","testable_assertion":"Given a non-Premium subscription in renewal, when the 3rd consecutive renewal attempt fails, the subscription MUST be automatically transitioned to 'cancelled' status."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state machine integrity constraint. The 'cancelled' state is terminal — there is no valid outbound transition. Allowing reactivation would break the subscription lifecycle model, create ambiguity around billing history continuity, proration, and whether old terms or new terms apply. It also has downstream implications for revenue recognition and audit trails. If the business wanted to allow reactivation, the entire state machine and its dependent accounting processes would need redesign — this isn't a parameter change.","testable_assertion":"Given a subscription in 'cancelled' status, any attempt to transition it to 'active' MUST be rejected. The system MUST enforce that the only path to active service is creating a new subscription entity."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 14-day duration and the choice to use a time-based grace window instead of attempt-counting are both business policy decisions tied to a pricing tier's value proposition. The grace period length could be changed to 21 days, or the Premium tier could be given 5 strikes instead, without corrupting system state. This is a differentiating feature of a tier, which is inherently a business-configurable policy.","testable_assertion":"Given a Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal, the subscription MUST NOT be cancelled until 14 days have elapsed since the first failed renewal attempt during that cycle, regardless of the number of retry attempts within that window."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency constraint that prevents abuse of the trial system. Allowing duplicate active trials per email would mean the system is in a logically inconsistent state — it is granting a benefit (free access) that was explicitly designed to be one-time. Unlike a policy parameter (e.g., trial length), this is a uniqueness constraint on entity creation. Violating it doesn't just change behavior — it corrupts the meaning of 'trial' as a concept in the domain.","testable_assertion":"Given an email address that already has an active free trial subscription, any attempt to create a second free trial subscription for that same email MUST be rejected."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer with a payment method that has status 'failed'","when":"The customer attempts to activate a subscription using that payment method","then":"The system rejects the activation and returns an error indicating the payment method must be valid"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An active subscription with a billing cycle ending on date X","when":"The current date reaches X minus 7 days","then":"The system initiates the auto-renewal process for that subscription"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A standard-tier subscription that has failed renewal 2 times already","when":"The 3rd renewal attempt also fails","then":"The subscription is automatically set to 'cancelled' status and the customer is notified"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A subscription in 'cancelled' status","when":"Any actor attempts to reactivate or transition it back to 'active'","then":"The system rejects the operation and enforces that a new subscription must be created"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a first failed renewal attempt at time T","when":"14 days have not yet elapsed since T","then":"The subscription remains active (or in a grace/past_due state) and is not cancelled regardless of how many retry attempts have failed"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a first failed renewal at time T","when":"14 days have elapsed since T and payment has still not succeeded","then":"The subscription is automatically cancelled"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An email address that already has one active free trial subscription","when":"A request is made to create another free trial for the same email","then":"The system rejects the request and returns an error indicating only one active trial per email is allowed"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"An email address with a previously completed or cancelled free trial","when":"A request is made to create a new free trial for that email","then":"The spec does not clarify whether repeat trials are allowed after the first one ends — this needs clarification (see escalations)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Payment method fails between subscription creation and activation","expected_behavior":"The activation must re-validate payment method status at the moment of activation, not rely on status at creation time."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber's payment succeeds on day 13 of the grace period","expected_behavior":"The subscription remains active; the grace period resets for the next billing cycle. No cancellation occurs."},{"scenario":"3rd renewal attempt fails but a manual payment is made immediately after","expected_behavior":"Unclear — if the subscription was already auto-cancelled per BR-3, the customer cannot reactivate per BR-4. The payment should be refunded or credited toward a new subscription."},{"scenario":"Customer changes email address while a free trial is active","expected_behavior":"The trial uniqueness constraint should track the original email. If the system allows email changes, the constraint enforcement mechanism matters — needs clarification."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber downgrades to standard tier during a grace period","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether the 14-day grace period continues or immediately switches to the 3-strike rule. Tier change during failure recovery is underspecified."},{"scenario":"Renewal initiated 7 days early but billing cycle is shorter than 7 days","expected_behavior":"For billing cycles shorter than 7 days (e.g., weekly plans), the renewal window overlaps or exceeds the entire cycle. The 7-day rule may need a floor or percentage-based alternative."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Incorrect enforcement of BR-1 or BR-6 directly impacts revenue leakage (failed payments creating active subs) and trial abuse (unlimited free access). The grace period policy for Premium affects churn of highest-value customers.","operational":"The 7-day renewal window and retry logic drive payment processing volume and dunning workflow complexity. The terminal cancellation rule (BR-4) simplifies subscription state management but requires robust new-subscription onboarding.","user":"The no-reactivation rule (BR-4) creates friction for customers who want to return. The Premium grace period is a significant retention benefit that differentiates tier value. Trial limits may frustrate users who feel they didn't get a fair evaluation period.","risk":"The biggest risk is the interaction between BR-3 and BR-4: automatic cancellation after 3 failures is irreversible, which could permanently lose customers due to temporary payment issues (expired card, bank hold). Premium's 14-day grace partially mitigates this for high-value customers.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (Standard tier)","impact":"Subject to aggressive 3-strike cancellation with no reactivation path — temporary payment issues can result in permanent service loss and need to re-subscribe."},{"group":"Customers (Premium tier)","impact":"Better protected by 14-day grace period, reinforcing premium value proposition."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Terminal cancellation rule affects MRR reporting, churn metrics, and cohort analysis. New subscriptions from returning customers must be tracked separately from retained subscribers."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will handle complaints from cancelled subscribers who want reactivation. Must be trained that reactivation is impossible and guide customers to create new subscriptions."},{"group":"Payment Operations","impact":"Must manage retry scheduling within the 7-day window and grace period, coordinate with payment processors on optimal retry timing."}]},"confidence":{"level":72,"reasoning":"The spec is relatively well-defined for core lifecycle rules. Confidence is above the escalation threshold because the rules themselves are clear and non-contradictory. Deducted points for underspecified interactions between rules (Premium downgrade during grace, repeat trials for same email after completion, short billing cycles).","high_confidence_areas":["Subscription cannot activate with failed payment (clear precondition)","Terminal cancellation state (clear state machine rule)","One active trial per email (clear uniqueness constraint)","3-strike cancellation for standard tier (clear threshold)"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether 'one trial per email' means one EVER or one ACTIVE at a time — the spec says 'at any time' suggesting concurrent limit only, but business intent likely includes preventing serial trial abuse","What happens during tier changes mid-grace-period","How the 7-day renewal window interacts with billing cycles shorter than 7 days","Whether Premium grace period is calendar days or business days"],"assumptions":["'Failed payment method' means the payment method's current stored status, validated at activation time","The 3 failed attempts in BR-3 are consecutive attempts within a single renewal cycle, not cumulative across the subscription lifetime","Premium grace period of 14 days runs from the first failed attempt in a given renewal cycle","'At any time' in BR-6 means concurrently active, not lifetime limit — though the business may want the latter"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"BR-6 states 'one active trial per email at any time' — does this mean only one CONCURRENT active trial (allowing a new trial after the first ends), or one trial EVER per email address? The phrase 'at any time' is ambiguous: it could mean 'at any given point in time' (concurrent limit) or 'ever, at any time' (lifetime limit). Most SaaS businesses intend a lifetime limit to prevent abuse, but the spec literally only constrains active trials.","affected_stakeholders":["Product team","Growth/Marketing team","Customers exploring the platform"],"options":["Interpret as concurrent limit only — allow new trials after previous trial ends or is cancelled","Interpret as lifetime limit — one trial ever per email address, period","Implement concurrent limit with a configurable max-lifetime-trials parameter (default 1)"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product team. Recommend lifetime limit (one trial ever per email) as the default to prevent serial trial abuse, but implement as a configurable policy so marketing can grant exceptions."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The interaction between Premium grace period (BR-5) and the terminal cancellation rule (BR-4) during tier changes is unspecified. If a Premium subscriber downgrades to Standard during their 14-day grace period, does the grace period continue or does the 3-strike rule immediately apply? Similarly, if a Standard subscriber upgrades to Premium after 2 failed attempts, do they get a fresh grace period?","affected_stakeholders":["Premium subscribers","Billing operations","Customer support"],"options":["Grace period follows the tier at time of first failure (snapshot)","Grace period follows current tier (dynamic)","Tier changes are blocked during active renewal failure recovery"],"recommendation":"Recommend snapshot approach: the retry/grace policy is locked to the tier at the time of the first renewal failure in a given cycle. This prevents gaming (upgrade to get grace period after 2 strikes) and is fairest to the customer (downgrade doesn't retroactively punish)."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.text: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation) with payment validation, retry logic, tier-specific grace periods, and free trial constraints.","bounded_context":"Subscription Billing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Subscription","definition":"A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, progressing through states: trial, active, past_due, cancelled."},{"term":"Payment Method","definition":"A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees. Has a status of valid or failed."},{"term":"Billing Cycle","definition":"The recurring period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when charges occur."},{"term":"Renewal","definition":"The automated process of charging a subscriber's payment method to extend the subscription for another billing cycle."},{"term":"Renewal Attempt","definition":"A single try to charge the payment method during the renewal window. Up to 3 attempts for standard tiers."},{"term":"Grace Period","definition":"A 14-day window granted to Premium tier subscribers before cancellation, replacing the 3-strike retry rule."},{"term":"Free Trial","definition":"A zero-cost introductory subscription period, limited to one active trial per email address."},{"term":"Premium Tier","definition":"A subscription level that receives preferential treatment during renewal failures (14-day grace period instead of 3 strikes)."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription with a known-bad payment method would create an immediately-corrupt state: an active subscription that cannot be billed. The system would be promising service it cannot collect revenue for. This is a precondition guard on a state transition, not a tunable policy — allowing it would violate the fundamental contract that active subscriptions have a viable billing path.","testable_assertion":"Given a payment method with status 'failed', any attempt to activate a subscription using that payment method MUST be rejected and the subscription MUST NOT transition to 'active'."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 7-day lead time is a business policy choice that balances retry window length against billing timing. A VP could reasonably change this to 5 or 10 days based on payment processor SLAs, retry success rate data, or customer feedback. The system would not be in a corrupt state if this window changed — it is an operational tuning parameter.","testable_assertion":"Given an active subscription, the renewal process MUST be initiated exactly 7 days before the current billing cycle's end date."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The threshold of 3 attempts is a business policy decision reflecting a tolerance level for payment failures. This number could be changed to 2 or 5 based on churn analysis, payment processor retry recommendations, or competitive benchmarking. The system state remains consistent regardless of the specific threshold. Note: the existence of a cancellation-after-exhaustion mechanism could be argued as invariant, but the specific count of 3 is clearly policy.","testable_assertion":"Given a non-Premium subscription in renewal, when the 3rd consecutive renewal attempt fails, the subscription MUST be automatically transitioned to 'cancelled' status."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state machine integrity constraint. The 'cancelled' state is terminal — there is no valid outbound transition. Allowing reactivation would break the subscription lifecycle model, create ambiguity around billing history continuity, proration, and whether old terms or new terms apply. It also has downstream implications for revenue recognition and audit trails. If the business wanted to allow reactivation, the entire state machine and its dependent accounting processes would need redesign — this isn't a parameter change.","testable_assertion":"Given a subscription in 'cancelled' status, any attempt to transition it to 'active' MUST be rejected. The system MUST enforce that the only path to active service is creating a new subscription entity."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 14-day duration and the choice to use a time-based grace window instead of attempt-counting are both business policy decisions tied to a pricing tier's value proposition. The grace period length could be changed to 21 days, or the Premium tier could be given 5 strikes instead, without corrupting system state. This is a differentiating feature of a tier, which is inherently a business-configurable policy.","testable_assertion":"Given a Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal, the subscription MUST NOT be cancelled until 14 days have elapsed since the first failed renewal attempt during that cycle, regardless of the number of retry attempts within that window."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency constraint that prevents abuse of the trial system. Allowing duplicate active trials per email would mean the system is in a logically inconsistent state — it is granting a benefit (free access) that was explicitly designed to be one-time. Unlike a policy parameter (e.g., trial length), this is a uniqueness constraint on entity creation. Violating it doesn't just change behavior — it corrupts the meaning of 'trial' as a concept in the domain.","testable_assertion":"Given an email address that already has an active free trial subscription, any attempt to create a second free trial subscription for that same email MUST be rejected."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer with a payment method that has status 'failed'","when":"The customer attempts to activate a subscription using that payment method","then":"The system rejects the activation and returns an error indicating the payment method must be valid"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An active subscription with a billing cycle ending on date X","when":"The current date reaches X minus 7 days","then":"The system initiates the auto-renewal process for that subscription"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A standard-tier subscription that has failed renewal 2 times already","when":"The 3rd renewal attempt also fails","then":"The subscription is automatically set to 'cancelled' status and the customer is notified"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A subscription in 'cancelled' status","when":"Any actor attempts to reactivate or transition it back to 'active'","then":"The system rejects the operation and enforces that a new subscription must be created"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a first failed renewal attempt at time T","when":"14 days have not yet elapsed since T","then":"The subscription remains active (or in a grace/past_due state) and is not cancelled regardless of how many retry attempts have failed"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a first failed renewal at time T","when":"14 days have elapsed since T and payment has still not succeeded","then":"The subscription is automatically cancelled"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An email address that already has one active free trial subscription","when":"A request is made to create another free trial for the same email","then":"The system rejects the request and returns an error indicating only one active trial per email is allowed"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"An email address with a previously completed or cancelled free trial","when":"A request is made to create a new free trial for that email","then":"The spec does not clarify whether repeat trials are allowed after the first one ends — this needs clarification (see escalations)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Payment method fails between subscription creation and activation","expected_behavior":"The activation must re-validate payment method status at the moment of activation, not rely on status at creation time."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber's payment succeeds on day 13 of the grace period","expected_behavior":"The subscription remains active; the grace period resets for the next billing cycle. No cancellation occurs."},{"scenario":"3rd renewal attempt fails but a manual payment is made immediately after","expected_behavior":"Unclear — if the subscription was already auto-cancelled per BR-3, the customer cannot reactivate per BR-4. The payment should be refunded or credited toward a new subscription."},{"scenario":"Customer changes email address while a free trial is active","expected_behavior":"The trial uniqueness constraint should track the original email. If the system allows email changes, the constraint enforcement mechanism matters — needs clarification."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber downgrades to standard tier during a grace period","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether the 14-day grace period continues or immediately switches to the 3-strike rule. Tier change during failure recovery is underspecified."},{"scenario":"Renewal initiated 7 days early but billing cycle is shorter than 7 days","expected_behavior":"For billing cycles shorter than 7 days (e.g., weekly plans), the renewal window overlaps or exceeds the entire cycle. The 7-day rule may need a floor or percentage-based alternative."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Incorrect enforcement of BR-1 or BR-6 directly impacts revenue leakage (failed payments creating active subs) and trial abuse (unlimited free access). The grace period policy for Premium affects churn of highest-value customers.","operational":"The 7-day renewal window and retry logic drive payment processing volume and dunning workflow complexity. The terminal cancellation rule (BR-4) simplifies subscription state management but requires robust new-subscription onboarding.","user":"The no-reactivation rule (BR-4) creates friction for customers who want to return. The Premium grace period is a significant retention benefit that differentiates tier value. Trial limits may frustrate users who feel they didn't get a fair evaluation period.","risk":"The biggest risk is the interaction between BR-3 and BR-4: automatic cancellation after 3 failures is irreversible, which could permanently lose customers due to temporary payment issues (expired card, bank hold). Premium's 14-day grace partially mitigates this for high-value customers.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (Standard tier)","impact":"Subject to aggressive 3-strike cancellation with no reactivation path — temporary payment issues can result in permanent service loss and need to re-subscribe."},{"group":"Customers (Premium tier)","impact":"Better protected by 14-day grace period, reinforcing premium value proposition."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Terminal cancellation rule affects MRR reporting, churn metrics, and cohort analysis. New subscriptions from returning customers must be tracked separately from retained subscribers."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will handle complaints from cancelled subscribers who want reactivation. Must be trained that reactivation is impossible and guide customers to create new subscriptions."},{"group":"Payment Operations","impact":"Must manage retry scheduling within the 7-day window and grace period, coordinate with payment processors on optimal retry timing."}]},"confidence":{"level":72,"reasoning":"The spec is relatively well-defined for core lifecycle rules. Confidence is above the escalation threshold because the rules themselves are clear and non-contradictory. Deducted points for underspecified interactions between rules (Premium downgrade during grace, repeat trials for same email after completion, short billing cycles).","high_confidence_areas":["Subscription cannot activate with failed payment (clear precondition)","Terminal cancellation state (clear state machine rule)","One active trial per email (clear uniqueness constraint)","3-strike cancellation for standard tier (clear threshold)"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether 'one trial per email' means one EVER or one ACTIVE at a time — the spec says 'at any time' suggesting concurrent limit only, but business intent likely includes preventing serial trial abuse","What happens during tier changes mid-grace-period","How the 7-day renewal window interacts with billing cycles shorter than 7 days","Whether Premium grace period is calendar days or business days"],"assumptions":["'Failed payment method' means the payment method's current stored status, validated at activation time","The 3 failed attempts in BR-3 are consecutive attempts within a single renewal cycle, not cumulative across the subscription lifetime","Premium grace period of 14 days runs from the first failed attempt in a given renewal cycle","'At any time' in BR-6 means concurrently active, not lifetime limit — though the business may want the latter"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"BR-6 states 'one active trial per email at any time' — does this mean only one CONCURRENT active trial (allowing a new trial after the first ends), or one trial EVER per email address? The phrase 'at any time' is ambiguous: it could mean 'at any given point in time' (concurrent limit) or 'ever, at any time' (lifetime limit). Most SaaS businesses intend a lifetime limit to prevent abuse, but the spec literally only constrains active trials.","affected_stakeholders":["Product team","Growth/Marketing team","Customers exploring the platform"],"options":["Interpret as concurrent limit only — allow new trials after previous trial ends or is cancelled","Interpret as lifetime limit — one trial ever per email address, period","Implement concurrent limit with a configurable max-lifetime-trials parameter (default 1)"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product team. Recommend lifetime limit (one trial ever per email) as the default to prevent serial trial abuse, but implement as a configurable policy so marketing can grant exceptions."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The interaction between Premium grace period (BR-5) and the terminal cancellation rule (BR-4) during tier changes is unspecified. If a Premium subscriber downgrades to Standard during their 14-day grace period, does the grace period continue or does the 3-strike rule immediately apply? Similarly, if a Standard subscriber upgrades to Premium after 2 failed attempts, do they get a fresh grace period?","affected_stakeholders":["Premium subscribers","Billing operations","Customer support"],"options":["Grace period follows the tier at time of first failure (snapshot)","Grace period follows current tier (dynamic)","Tier changes are blocked during active renewal failure recovery"],"recommendation":"Recommend snapshot approach: the retry/grace policy is locked to the tier at the time of the first renewal failure in a given cycle. This prevents gaming (upgrade to get grace period after 2 strikes) and is fairest to the customer (downgrade doesn't retroactively punish)."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
